title: The road kept opening east
oneLineMemory: A transatlantic jump became a week measured in walks, long roads, and a return arc from Iceland.

chapters.title: Across the ocean
chapters.summary: The route leaves Home, crosses the Atlantic by air, and reaches Reykjavík in the evening.

chapters.title: A city at walking pace
chapters.summary: Nearly two days center on Reykjavík, including an observed walking trace through the city.

chapters.title: The long road east
chapters.summary: The journey follows the south coast through Vík í Mýrdal to Jökulsárlón, then turns back west.

chapters.title: The homeward arc
chapters.summary: One final night in Reykjavík precedes the flight and ground route back Home.

highlights.title: Leaving the home region
highlights.description: A 5,334 km flight begins the main geographic transition in the record.

highlights.title: Turning toward the south coast
highlights.description: The route changes from city-scale movement to a 187 km drive toward Vík í Mýrdal.

highlights.title: Farthest east
highlights.description: The observed trace reaches Jökulsárlón after the longest ground leg of the journey.

highlights.title: Back inside the home radius
highlights.description: A sustained Home visit closes the detected trip boundary.

captions.text: The map pulls wide: 5,334 km across the Atlantic.
For a morning, the record slows to walking pace.
Three and a half hours east, the route reaches the lagoon.
The final line closes at Home.

reflectionQuestions.question: When you think of the long drive east from Vík, what detail returns first?
reflectionQuestions.reason: The Timeline records the route and duration, but not what made that stretch memorable to you.

reflectionQuestions.question: What were you looking for—or avoiding—during the Reykjavík walk?
reflectionQuestions.reason: The observed path shows a deliberate two-hour walk, but its purpose is absent from the data.

uncertaintyNotes: The brief roadside stop near Vík has low-confidence location evidence, so no activity is assigned to it.
The route establishes where and when movement occurred; it does not identify companions, purpose, or emotion.
